implementation_drift.description: Multiple substring corrections: 'plasticity loss' phrase strict-substring is rare in literature where 'Loss of Plasticity' is the common phrasing. Strict count produces 0 matches.

scientific_taste.description: Mechanical rule produces PASS but I do not think this candidate is paradigm-shift. The substantive prior art is dense (Nature paper on Loss of Plasticity in Deep Continual Learning; 2402.18762; 2602.09234). The strict-substring 0-match is an artifact of word-order — 'Loss of Plasticity' has the words but not in the same order as 'plasticity loss'. My honest assessment: this is NOT a real PASS. Flagging for human review. Did NOT change content_words mid-round to fix.

honest_note: CRITICAL: mechanical rule says PASS but substantively this IS a saturated candidate. Strict-substring 'plasticity loss' is not in 'Loss of Plasticity' because of word order. The verdict is a strict-substring artifact. I am following the mechanical rule (as required by the program) and recording PASS, but flagging in scientific_taste that this is not a real PASS. The cross-agent verifier should catch this.
